turns.text: Hello Charolette, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're thinking about starting a family, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in seat, tucking a strand of hair behind my ear*

Yeah, okay. I appreciate you saying that. I'm... I'm still getting used to talking about this stuff, honestly. It's not exactly something you lead conversations with at school, you know?

*takes a breath*

So, I guess the short version is that I lost my first pregnancy pretty early on. It was about two years ago now, and I thought I'd be further along in processing it by now, but— *pauses* —it still hits different some days. And now I'm trying again, or at least... preparing to try again. My body's ready, or at least the labs are saying it is. My brain's a different story.

*looks down at hands*

What makes this complicated is that I'm kind of doing this alone. No partner, and my family's not really here in Salem. I'm a nursing student, so I understand what's happening medically, which sounds like it should help, but honestly? Sometimes it makes it worse. I know all the things that can go wrong. I obsess over the statistics. And then I feel guilty for not being more grateful or optimistic, you know?

The doctors here are fine. They're competent. But it's exhausting being treated like I'm just a "case" sometimes instead of... *gestures vaguely* ...a person who's scared and hoping this time will be different.

I want this so badly though. That part's very real.
Tell me about the time when you first started thinking seriously about having a baby — what was going on in your life, and what did that feel like?

*leans back, eyes getting a bit distant*

God, that's... that's actually a hard question because it wasn't like I sat down and made this rational decision, you know? It was more like — *pauses* — I realized I wanted something I couldn't control, and that terrified me.

I was maybe twenty-one when I first got pregnant. It wasn't planned. I was working a part-time job, trying to balance school, and when I found out... *voice gets quieter* ...I was actually excited. Like, scared out of my mind, but excited. I started picturing things. I had this whole imaginary future mapped out in my head in about five minutes.

*fidgets with sleeve*

And then it just... ended. Before I even got to see anything on an ultrasound. The grief was weird because I felt like I wasn't supposed to be *that* devastated, you know? Like, it didn't even count because it was so early. But it counted to me. It counted a lot.

After that, I think I went into this shutdown phase for a while. Like, "okay, clearly I'm not ready." But the thing is — *meets your eyes* — as much as that loss broke something in me, it also made me realize how much I actually want to be a mother. Not someday in this abstract future, but like... now. Actually soon.

So now I'm here, trying again, and it's this strange mix of hope and absolute dread. Does that make sense?
Tell me about how you went about preparing — or thinking about preparing — for pregnancy. What steps, if any, did you take, and what guided those choices?

*rubs forehead*

Okay, so this is where my nursing brain actually comes in handy, I guess. I knew what I was *supposed* to do — prenatal vitamins, folic acid, get my titers checked, make sure my iron levels are good. All that stuff. And I did do those things. I got the bloodwork done, started taking vitamins religiously. It's like... the one part of this I can actually control, right? The biology.

*shifts uncomfortably*

But honestly? A lot of my "preparation" has been less about the medical stuff and more about just... trying to get my life stable enough to handle a pregnancy. I'm still figuring out employment — like, full-time work that actually pays decent and has benefits. That's been the real nightmare. You can't just *want* to be pregnant and healthy; you need money, insurance, a job that won't fire you for taking time off.

*voice gets a bit frustrated*

And then there's the transportation thing. I take the bus to the clinic, and it's a twenty-minute walk to the stop. I keep thinking about what that looks like when I'm pregnant, or if I'm having complications and need to get there fast. These are things the doctors don't really ask about, but they're constantly in my head.

*looks at you directly*

The mental preparation though... that's been harder. I've tried to talk to people about my last loss, but I'm kind of isolated here. So a lot of it is just me, alone in my apartment, reading medical journals when I should probably be doing something healthier for my anxiety.
Where did you go to find out what you needed to know — friends, family, online, a doctor?

*laughs a little, but there's an edge to it*

That's the thing though — I don't really have a "friends and family" network here in Salem. My family's scattered, and we're not super close anyway. They mean well, but they're not... present, you know? So I can't exactly call my mom and ask her about this stuff.

*taps fingers on knee*

A lot of it has been online, honestly. Which is both a blessing and a curse. I'll go down these Reddit rabbit holes at like midnight, reading other people's miscarriage stories, and then I'm spiraling. I've looked at pregnancy forums, parenting blogs, medical journals when I can access them through school. My nursing textbooks too — I reread the obstetrics sections more times than is probably healthy.

*voice gets quieter*

As for the doctors here at the clinic, they give me information when I ask. The thing is, because I'm a nursing student, they kind of... assume I already know stuff? Or they go into shorthand with me. Which sometimes is fine, but other times I just want someone to sit down and actually *talk* through it with me, not just hand me a pamphlet or go through a checklist.

*looks down*

I think what I'm missing is someone who's actually *in my life*, you know? Someone I could call at 2 AM when I'm freaking out. Instead, I'm googling things and trying to piece together what applies to me specifically. It's lonely, and I know I'm not getting the best information that way.
Tell me about any conversations you had with a GP, midwife, or other health professional before you became pregnant. What was that like?

*settles back, thinking*

So I did go see someone at the clinic when I decided I was ready to try again. It was... fine? Clinical, mostly. They did the standard preconception stuff — asked about my medical history, my family history, whether I smoke or drink. The usual screening questions.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

What I remember most is that it felt very checkbox-y, if that makes sense. Like, they went through their list, I answered, and then we were done. They prescribed the prenatal vitamins, ordered the labs — titers, iron, all of it. Very efficient.

*voice becomes a bit more hesitant*

But nobody really... asked about my previous loss in a meaningful way? It was just noted in the chart. "Previous pregnancy, ended at X weeks." And then we moved on. I wanted to talk about it, about my fears that it might happen again, but the appointment was already running over, and I could tell they had other patients waiting.

*rubs arm*

The hardest part was that nobody asked about my actual life situation. Like, they didn't say, "How are you getting here? Do you have support at home? Are you in a stable place right now?" It's all physical health, no context. And I get it — they're busy, they have protocols — but I felt very... invisible in that room. Just a chart with some flags on it.

*looks at you*

I wish someone had asked if I was actually okay, you know?
Tell me about what it was like to try to conceive — however long or short that period was for you. What stands out most from that time?

*takes a shaky breath*

Okay, so I'm still in that phase actually. I haven't gotten pregnant again yet, which... *voice wavers slightly* ...which is its own kind of torture, honestly.

*fidgets with hands*

What stands out most is the obsessiveness of it. I track everything — my cycle, my temperature, my mood, what I eat. I know exactly when I'm ovulating. I've probably taken more pregnancy tests than most people take in a lifetime, and I'm only a few months in. Every single month, there's this moment of hope, and then... nothing. And it's like reliving that loss over and over.

*voice gets quieter, more vulnerable*

The worst part is the silence around it. I can't exactly tell my classmates, "Hey, I'm trying to get pregnant and it's not working," because then it becomes this thing. And at work — well, when I find stable work — people are going to ask questions eventually anyway. It's this secret I'm carrying alone.

*looks up*

There's also this weird guilt that comes with it. Like, am I doing enough? Am I eating right, exercising enough, not stressing too much? But stress is inevitable when you're stressed about being stressed, right? *gives a weak smile*

And then there's the fear that my body's broken. That it failed me once and it'll fail me again. Some days I'm optimistic, telling myself it was just bad luck. Other days I'm convinced something's fundamentally wrong and nobody's found it yet.

It's... a lot. For one person.
Tell me about the picture you had in your mind of what pregnancy and becoming a parent would be like — where did those ideas come from?

*gets a faraway look, almost wistful*

Oh man. *laughs softly* I have a very specific picture in my head, and I know some of it is probably unrealistic, but I can't help it.

*leans forward slightly*

A lot of it comes from my nursing training, honestly. I'm drawn to neonatal care — like, that's the specialty I want to go into eventually. So in my head, I picture myself as this competent, capable mother who understands her baby's needs on a clinical level but also just... loves fiercely, you know? I imagine being able to advocate for my kid in medical settings because I speak the language. I thought that would be my superpower.

*voice softens*

But there's also this quieter, more personal picture. I imagine having someone who is mine. Like, biologically and legally and emotionally mine. Someone who looks at me like I'm their whole world. I think about teaching them things, watching them grow. I know that sounds generic, but — *pauses* — after feeling so alone here, the idea of not being alone anymore is... it's powerful.

*becomes more hesitant*

Where it gets complicated is that a lot of my picture assumes I'll have figured out other stuff by then. A stable job, maybe a partner eventually, financial security. But I'm trying to get pregnant *before* those pieces are in place, and I'm aware of how backwards that is. 

*meets your eyes*

I think deep down, part of me believes that having a baby will fix things. That it'll give my life purpose and direction. And I know that's not fair to put on a child, but... it's what I feel.
How much were those ideas shaped by your own family's experiences — your mother's, or others close to you?

*shifts uncomfortably, looking away*

That's... yeah, that's complicated.

*takes a moment*

My mom had me young — like, really young. She was nineteen. And honestly? She did her best, but it was hard on her. She's told me stories about how tough it was, how she felt like she missed out on things. She didn't say it to guilt me or anything, but it was always there in the background — this cautionary tale about what happens when you have a baby before you're ready.

*voice becomes quieter*

Which is ironic because here I am at twenty-three, doing basically the same thing she did, except at least she had my dad in the picture, even if they didn't last. I don't even have that.

*rubs face*

I think part of why I want this so badly is actually because of her, in a weird way. Seeing her struggle, seeing how hard she worked — it made me respect motherhood. But it also made me think, "I'm going to do this differently. I'm going to be more prepared, more educated, more stable." Except I'm not stable, and I'm not sure how prepared you can actually be.

*looks at you*

The other family stuff... *hesitates* ...we're not close enough for them to have shaped my parenting ideas much. Which is lonely, but also maybe a blessing? I don't have a lot of family baggage to unpack, but I also don't have a template to follow. Or anyone to call when I'm freaking out.

*quiet voice*

I think I'm kind of making this up as I go.
Tell me about any financial, practical, or life circumstances that were part of the picture as you were thinking about having a baby.

*lets out a long breath*

Okay, so this is the part where my medical knowledge doesn't help at all, because I understand the risks of poverty on pregnancy outcomes, and I'm living it.

*rubs temples*

The job situation is probably the biggest stress. I'm still a student, which means I'm piecing together part-time work when I can find it. Nothing stable. No benefits. And I keep thinking about maternity leave — like, how do you take maternity leave when you don't have a real job? How do you afford childcare on nothing?

*voice gets tight*

Then there's housing. I have my apartment here in Salem, which is fine, but it's not exactly a family-friendly space, and the rent takes up more of my income than it should. If I have a baby, I'd need to move, get something bigger. That costs money I don't have.

*counts on fingers*

Transportation — I already mentioned this, but it's constant. The bus, the twenty-minute walk. What happens in winter when I'm pregnant and exhausted? What if there's an emergency and I need to get to the hospital fast?

*looks down*

And then there's just... stuff. Diapers, formula if I can't breastfeed, clothes, medical care. I know the statistics on how expensive kids are, and I lie awake at night doing the math, trying to figure out how this is even possible.

*voice wavers*

Honestly? Sometimes I think the only way this works is if I'm delusional enough to believe it will somehow figure itself out. Because the rational part of me knows I'm not ready. But I want this anyway.
Were there things you felt you needed to have 'in place' before starting, and did that match what actually happened?

*laughs bitterly*

Oh God, yeah. I had this whole list in my head.

*counts on fingers with heavy sarcasm*

I was going to have stable full-time employment with benefits. I was going to have saved up at least three months of expenses. I was going to finish at least another year of nursing school so I'd be closer to graduating. I was going to have a car so I wouldn't be dependent on the bus system. Maybe even... *voice gets quieter* ...a partner, or at least not be completely alone.

*slumps back*

None of that happened. Well, I'm still working on the school part, but everything else? Stalled.

*rubs face*

What actually happened is I got tired of waiting. Like, intellectually I know all the reasons to wait. I can recite them. But emotionally? After that first loss, something shifted. I realized I could do everything "right" and still lose a pregnancy. So why not just... try? Why put my life on hold indefinitely?

*voice becomes more raw*

And maybe that's selfish. Maybe I'm being irresponsible. But I'm also twenty-three, and I'm terrified my window is closing somehow, even though I know that's not logical. I'm scared I'll never get pregnant again. I'm scared if I wait too long, something else will go wrong.

*looks at you directly*

So no, nothing matched. I just started trying anyway, knowing I wasn't ready. And now I'm living with that decision every single day.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*pauses, thinking hard, eyes getting a bit watery*

Yeah, actually. There is something.

*voice gets smaller*

I wish someone would acknowledge how isolating this is. Like, I know I'm not the only woman going through this, but it feels that way. The clinic treats me like a case number. My classmates don't understand because they're not thinking about babies yet. My family's not here. And society's messaging is so weird — like, you're supposed to want this, but also have your life perfect first, but also your biological clock is ticking, but also don't be selfish...

*wipes eyes*

I think what I needed, especially after my loss, was someone to actually *see* me. Not as a nursing student who should know better, not as a statistic on a risk assessment, not as a woman who's making questionable choices. Just... see me as someone who's grieving and hoping and terrified all at the same time.

*takes a shaky breath*

And maybe... maybe I needed someone to ask if I was okay before asking about my labs. To talk about my fears, not just my iron levels. Because the medical stuff, I can handle that. But the emotional part — the loneliness, the guilt, the pressure I'm putting on myself — nobody's really addressing that.

*looks at you*

I don't even know what the answer is. But I know that matters. It matters that I'm not okay, and pretending everything's clinical doesn't change that.
